findings.message: `FindSimilarArgs` leaves both `fragment` and `file` optional, so `sotp find-similar` with no query parses successfully and only fails later in `resolve_fragment_text`. The briefing explicitly assigns this layer the `find-similar <fragment>` / `--file` parse contract, so the CLI help/usage and validation are currently out of sync with AC-01.

`dup-index measure-quality` is specified here as a `--workspace-root` command, but this CLI adds and forwards `--db-path` for that subcommand. That leaks an irrelevant index-path dependency into AC-03: the downstream measure-quality wiring opens the DB path before computing metrics, so a bad or unwritable path can make the command fail even though the quality calculation is defined only from workspace fragments.

P1: [apps/cli/src/commands/semantic_dup.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/apps/cli/src/commands/semantic_dup.rs#L23) lets both `fragment` and `file` be omitted, so `sotp find-similar` parses successfully with no query and only fails later in `resolve_fragment_text`. That is out of sync with the briefing’s required fragment-or-`--file` parse contract for AC-01.

P1: [apps/cli/src/commands/semantic_dup.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/apps/cli/src/commands/semantic_dup.rs#L86) adds `--db-path` to `dup-index measure-quality`, but the briefing only specifies `--workspace-root` for that command. Because the CLI composition path opens the DB before computing metrics, an invalid or unwritable DB path can fail an otherwise valid quality run.

`read_lines_file` takes `&PathBuf`, which triggers Clippy's default `ptr_arg` warning. This repo runs Clippy with `-D warnings` in `clippy-local`/`ci-rust`, so the new CLI scope will fail the required gate even though the code would compile. Change the helper to accept `&Path` and import/use `std::path::Path`.

The ADR selects LanceDB as the first candidate stack in D2, but the reassessment trigger describes hitting sqlite-vec-style full-scan limits as a reason for a future migration to `LanceDB / USearch`. That makes the rollout baseline ambiguous: readers cannot tell whether Phase 1 starts on LanceDB as decided or on sqlite-vec with LanceDB deferred.

T007 assigns the workspace scanner/extractor to `BuildIndexInteractor`, but the usecase design only gives interactors `EmbeddingPort` and `SemanticIndexPort` and `BuildIndexCommand` only carries `workspace_root`. With no fragment-extraction port or pre-extracted fragments in the command, implementation must either put filesystem scanning inside the usecase interactor, contradicting the stated pure-orchestrator/no-I/O hexagonal design, or leave `dup-index build` without a way to obtain fragments.

IN-01/IN-02 name the port implementations as `EmbeddingAdapter` and `SemanticIndexAdapter`, but the same design later declares the ports as `EmbeddingPort` and `SemanticIndexPort` in IN-07 and the usecase catalogue. This conflates adapter and port names at the spec layer, so the infrastructure tasks are grounded in the wrong named design elements.

T009 still says `dup-check` accepts diff fragments via `--file <path>` while the section narrative and the same sentence say the concrete interface is `--files-from <newline-separated-file>`. That leaves the required CLI surface contradictory for the implementation task.

T003 requires `BuildIndexInteractor` to walk the filesystem and extract `CodeFragment` values, but T007 is later defined as the task that implements the scanner/extractor that `BuildIndexInteractor` calls. With T007 depending on the T003 interface, T003 cannot be completed as written without doing T007's work first or duplicating it.

MeasureQualityInteractor is specified as a pure orchestrator that delegates entirely to EmbeddingPort and SemanticIndexPort, but the measure-quality command/API is driven by a workspace_root and even has an Io error variant. With no fragment-extraction port, pre-extracted fragments, or helper task for measure-quality, the implementation must either do filesystem scanning inside this supposedly pure interactor or cannot obtain the Rust source fragments needed for AC-03.

SimilarityScore is defined as a cosine similarity score constrained to [0.0, 1.0], and the plan tests rejection of negative values, but raw cosine similarity can be negative and AC-03 requires measuring the cosine similarity distribution. This makes valid cosine results unrepresentable unless the design explicitly normalizes them before constructing SimilarityScore.

The measure-quality design promises a false_positive_rate, but MeasureQualityCommand only supplies workspace_root and T009 only wires --workspace-root. A false-positive rate requires a definition of positives/negatives or labelled/evaluation input; with only an unlabeled workspace the task can output a score distribution, but not a meaningful false-positive rate for the ADR's PoC decision.

T007 places the recursive Rust-file scanner in `libs/usecase/src/semantic_dup.rs` and has the interactors perform filesystem traversal. That is a wrong layer placement for this repository's hexagonal convention: usecase is supposed to stay an orchestration layer and filesystem I/O belongs outside it or behind an infrastructure port. Implementing this plan as written will bake a layer violation into the implementation plan.

The plan says T007 depends only on T001, but the same task also surfaces failures as `BuildIndexError::Io` and replaces the `BuildIndexInteractor` stub. Those symbols are introduced by T002/T003, so T007 cannot be implemented or tested as a T001-only task. The dependency ordering is internally inconsistent and can send implementers into an impossible task split.

T007 requires the workspace scanner/extractor to live in `libs/usecase/src/semantic_dup.rs` and use `std::fs`, and states that this is accepted. That contradicts the required hexagonal convention's Usecase Layer Purity Rules, which explicitly forbid `std::fs::*` in usecase and direct file reading to CLI or an adapter boundary. Implementing the plan as written would bake in a usecase-purity/layer violation.

S2 says `MeasureQualityInteractor` is a pure orchestrator that delegates entirely to `EmbeddingPort` and `SemanticIndexPort`, but T003 later says it walks `MeasureQualityCommand.workspace_root` to extract fragments. With no extraction port or pre-extracted fragments in the command, the plan is internally contradictory about whether this interactor performs filesystem scanning or remains a pure orchestrator.

`BuildIndexInteractor` is still documented as scanning workspace Rust files, but the plan explicitly says all interactors are pure usecase orchestrators with no filesystem I/O and that extraction is done by the infrastructure helper before constructing `BuildIndexCommand`. If implementers follow this catalogue text, the usecase layer will own filesystem scanning, contradicting the hexagonal split fixed elsewhere in the plan.

T001 says `SemanticDupError` should be implemented with `Debug/Clone/Copy/Display/Error` “as specified in domain-types.json”, but the domain catalogue only declares `Debug`, `Display`, and `Error` for `SemanticDupError`. Following the task text would add trait implementations that the catalogue does not specify, so the implementation plan and the type contract disagree.

AC-01 requires `sotp find-similar` to let callers specify `k`, but the CLI task only lists parsing fragment text / `--file` for find-similar and `--workspace-root` for build. Since T008 is the CLI wiring task that claims to cover AC-01, the implementation plan does not actually instruct implementers to expose the required top-k option.

AC-03/IN-05 require the quality measurement output to include the cosine similarity distribution and false-positive rate, but `QualityMetrics` only contains `mean_score` and `false_positive_rate`. A mean is not a distribution, so the type catalogue cannot satisfy the stated measurement output contract.

AC-03/IN-05 require the quality command to report the cosine similarity distribution for threshold calibration, but `QualityMetrics` represents that distribution using `SimilarityScore`; `SimilarityScore` is defined as a clamped/normalized [0,1] value even though the same catalogue acknowledges raw cosine is [-1,1]. That means negative cosine values are collapsed before the mean/distribution is reported, so the plan no longer measures the cosine distribution it says it will measure.

`sotp find-similar` is specified to accept raw fragment text, and T008 constructs `FindSimilarCommand { fragment, top_k }` from that text, but the only fragment type is `CodeFragment` and it requires an originating `source_path`. The plan does not define a pathless query-fragment type or an explicit sentinel path, so the raw-text CLI contract is inconsistent with the type design and would require inventing fake source metadata.

AC-03/IN-05 require the quality command to output the cosine similarity distribution and false-positive rate, but `QualityMetrics` only carries mean, standard deviation, and a heuristic false-positive field. Since T009 only serializes these metrics, the plan cannot satisfy the stated measurement contract or provide the distribution needed for threshold calibration.

`false_positive_rate` is defined as the fraction of randomly sampled cross-file pairs above the threshold, treating them as non-duplicates. That is not a meaningful false-positive rate without labeled positives/negatives, so the plan's PoC metric does not actually measure the ADR/spec requirement it claims to cover.

AC-03/IN-05 require the quality command to report a false-positive rate, but the planned `QualityMetrics` only exposes `above_threshold_rate` and explicitly defines it as a proxy from unlabeled random cross-file pairs. Since T003/T009 only compute and serialize these metrics, the plan cannot satisfy the stated measurement contract unless it either adds labeled/evaluation input for a true false-positive rate or relaxes the spec to require the proxy metric instead.

The task-state-only briefing says there should be no catalogue/signal drift, but this generated domain type view now changes `SimilarFragment` to a fully signalled state, with `SemanticDupError` similarly changed on line 17. That is outside the requested T001 status bookkeeping change and should be reverted or moved to the implementation/signals scope.

The closing sentence overstates the license set: immediately above, `MPL-2.0` is explicitly classified as weak copyleft, and `RUSTSEC-2024-0436` is an advisory accept rather than a license. That makes the claim that all added items are "permissive or non-code-only" internally inconsistent.

The ADR says MPL-2.0 has no source-disclosure obligation when used unmodified, but MPL-2.0 source-availability obligations can still apply to MPL-covered files when distributing an executable/larger work even if those files were not modified. This is a semantic license-governance error in the consequence being recorded for the allowlist expansion.

This sentence says the CLI composition root in `T008/T009` extracts fragments using `T007`, but `T009`’s own task text says `dup-check` reads each file listed by `--files-from` directly into `CodeFragment` values. That makes the fragment-acquisition path inconsistent for `dup-check` and overstates `T007`’s role.

`T004` is now recorded as `done` with a commit hash, and the rendered plan marks it resolved, even though this plan-artifacts scope is described as only advancing `T002` and `T003` to `in_progress`. That makes the plan state claim completion of the infrastructure dependency task as part of unrelated T002/T003 bookkeeping, so the rendered task progress is semantically inconsistent with the scope/design intent.

The plan-artifact update is not limited to T005/T006 bookkeeping: the diff also changes T002 and T003 from `in_progress` to `done` and adds commit hashes, which contradicts the scope briefing's stated intent that only the T005/T006 status fields changed in `impl-plan.json`. This also drives the rendered `plan.md` resolved count and task checkboxes, so the artifacts no longer represent the described T005/T006-only update.

The `LanceDbSemanticIndexAdapter` row is marked 🔴 here, but the scope brief says the adapter structs are expected to remain 🟡 and that this view should have 0 red signals. That makes the rendered catalogue inconsistent with the intended bookkeeping state.

`LanceDbSemanticIndexAdapter` is marked `🔴` here, but this scope's intended state says the two adapter structs should remain `🟡` while only the four trait implementations flip to `🔵`. That makes the rendered catalogue inconsistent with the plan metadata for this round.

The plan-artifacts briefing says this scope is only T008/T009 bookkeeping, with only those two statuses moving to `in_progress`, but `impl-plan.json` also marks T007 as `done` and adds a `commit_hash`. That extra task-state transition changes the rendered plan to `7/11 resolved` and claims T007 completion, so the artifact no longer matches the intended bookkeeping scope for this change.

T007 is marked `done` with a `commit_hash`, but this plan-artifact update is scoped to T008/T009 bookkeeping only. That also drives the generated plan to show 7/11 resolved and T007 checked, so the artifact now records an extra task-state transition outside the described plan update.

The scope briefing says this bookkeeping change should only move T008 and T009 to `in_progress`, but `impl-plan.json` also marks T007 as `done` and adds a completion hash, with the generated plan rendering T007 as checked. That records an extra task completion outside the stated T008/T009 cycle; either revert T007 to its prior in-progress state in these artifacts or include/justify the T007 completion in the scope.

`dup_check` hard-codes `DUP_CHECK_TOP_K` to 10, so `SemanticIndexPort::search` can never return all above-threshold matches when more than 10 candidates qualify. That makes `DupCheckWarning.similar_fragments` incomplete and can suppress a warning that the soft gate should emit.

`measure_quality` evaluates every cross-file pair instead of using a random sample, but AC-03 defines the threshold-exceed metric as a random-sampled proxy. As written, the reported `above_threshold_rate` is biased and the routine scales as O(n^2) on larger workspaces.

The threshold boundary test name encodes the opposite expected result: `test_dup_check_interactor_produces_no_warning_when_score_equals_threshold` asserts one warning and its message says equality should warn. Because the project convention puts the expected result in the test name, this makes the boundary behavior contradictory in the test suite; rename it to the asserted behavior or change the assertion if equality should not warn.

The usecase test module adds only 14 `#[test]` functions, while the briefing/design intent for this scope says T010 adds 15 unit tests for the four interactors. That leaves the usecase test task incomplete unless the missing test is added or the scope intent is corrected.

`test_find_similar_interactor_delegates_embed_then_search` does not verify the actual delegation arguments: the `search` mock accepts any embedding and any `TopK`, so an implementation that ignores the embedding result or forwards the wrong `top_k` would still pass. This does not assert the intended embed-to-search delegation contract.

The new T010 tests leave exercisable public error paths untested: `DupCheckInteractor::dup_check` has no case for embedding failure or index search failure, and `BuildIndexInteractor::build_index` has no case for insert failure. Those services' public contracts promise error propagation, so regressions that swallow or mis-map these errors would still pass this test suite.

The only cross-file metrics test uses orthogonal embeddings and asserts a zero mean plus percentile length, so it would still pass if `measure_quality` skipped pair computation and returned the no-pairs zero metrics. This leaves the non-empty metrics path effectively untested; use a non-zero similarity case and assert the actual metric values.

This expectation accepts any fragment and any embedding for all three `insert` calls. An implementation that embeds three fragments but inserts the same fragment repeatedly, or inserts an embedding unrelated to the embedding port result, would still pass, so the test does not verify the intended per-fragment insert delegation.

The find-similar happy-path expectation ignores the `top_k` argument passed to `search`. If the interactor stopped forwarding the caller's requested `TopK`, this test would still pass, leaving part of the public delegation contract uncovered.

`test_build_index_interactor_calls_insert_for_each_fragment` captures only the embedding argument and ignores the `fragment` passed to `insert`. An implementation that embeds each input but inserts the same or wrong `CodeFragment` on every call would still pass, so the test does not verify the intended per-fragment insert delegation contract.

P1 test coverage gap: the cross-file `MeasureQualityInteractor` happy-path test only verifies `mean_cosine` and `cosine_percentiles`, but leaves the other public metrics (`cosine_std_dev` and `above_threshold_rate`) unchecked for the non-empty pair case. A regression that returns an incorrect standard deviation or threshold rate while preserving mean/percentiles would still pass this test, so the public API's metrics behavior is not fully covered as required.

The reservoir update is not Algorithm R: once `reservoir` reaches `MAX_SAMPLE`, every new similarity is unconditionally inserted and only a previously stored item is evicted. That makes the sample strongly biased toward later pairs, so the streaming estimates for large corpora are not uniform and can diverge materially from the intended sampled semantics.

`cosine_percentiles` are now computed from the reservoir rather than from the full set of pairwise similarities. Even if the reservoir sampler were fixed, this changes the percentile outputs for any corpus with more than `MAX_SAMPLE` pairs, whereas the previous implementation returned exact percentiles over all similarities.

`cosine_percentiles` is documented as the full cosine-similarity distribution, but this code now derives it from the `reservoir` sample once the corpus exceeds `MAX_SAMPLE`. That makes `QualityMetrics` approximate for large inputs and changes the public API's percentile values versus the previous exact implementation, which can materially skew threshold calibration.

The reservoir sampler is not actually uniform: `xorshift_state as usize % welford_count` introduces modulo bias, so replacement decisions are slightly skewed toward lower indices. That breaks the stated Algorithm R guarantee and can bias `cosine_percentiles` and `above_threshold_rate` on large corpora.

`semantic_dup_check` silently ignores `ack = true` when `ack_file` is `None`, so a public caller can request ack persistence and get a success result with no file written. That makes the exported DTO accept an illegal state and drops the user's `--ack` intent without an error. Reject this combination up front or make the invariant unrepresentable in the input type.

This destructive rebuild path has no regression test in the file for the new behavior: first-run no-op, repeated rebuild replacing stale rows, or propagation of `remove_dir_all` failures. Because `dup-index build` now deletes data before rebuilding, that coverage gap leaves the stale-index fix and the new failure mode unprotected from regression.

Deleting `input.db_path` here can wipe arbitrary user data if the CLI is invoked with an overlapping or misconfigured `--db-path` (for example, pointing at the workspace root or another real directory). Because this happens before fragment extraction, a single bad invocation can erase the project tree before the command fails. Reject unsafe path overlaps or constrain cleanup to a dedicated index directory before calling `remove_dir_all`.

This destructive rebuild path has no tests covering the new behavior: neither the happy path where an existing `--db-path` is wiped and rebuilt, nor the refusal path when `--db-path` overlaps the workspace root. Because this method now deletes data before adapter construction, the missing coverage leaves a high-risk regression surface unverified.

`is_recognizable_lancedb_index()` treats any existing `fragments.lance` entry as a valid LanceDB marker because it only checks `.exists()`. An unrelated directory that happens to contain a file or symlink with that name will be classified as an index and then recursively deleted by `remove_dir_all`, which defeats the new data-loss guard. This needs to verify the marker is the expected directory/layout, not just that some path exists.

`Path::is_dir()` follows symlinks, so an existing non-index directory that contains a `fragments.lance` symlink to any directory is accepted as a recognizable index and then `remove_dir_all(db_path)` deletes that whole user directory. This leaves a data-loss bypass of the new guard; use non-following metadata for the marker and reject symlinks.

If the rename of `temp_path` into `db_path` fails after the existing index has already been renamed aside, the function returns `Err` with `db_path` absent and only the `.old` backup left behind. That breaks the non-destructive rebuild guarantee and can make a transient filesystem error look like data loss.

The `fragment_count == 0` early return skips `commit_rebuilt_index`, so a rebuild of an emptied workspace leaves any pre-existing index in place. Because `dup-index build` is a rebuild command, this preserves stale semantic-index data instead of reflecting the current tree.

P1: `commit_rebuilt_index` does not actually guarantee the old index is preserved on a failed swap. After moving `db_path` to the backup, it ignores the result of restoring the backup if `rename(temp_path, db_path)` fails. If that restore also fails, the command returns an error with `db_path` still missing, which contradicts the stated non-destructive rebuild guarantee.

`commit_rebuilt_index` is not atomic as a whole: once `db_path` has been renamed to `.{filename}.old`, a crash, kill, or power loss before the `temp_path -> db_path` rename leaves `db_path` missing and only the hidden backup remains. The rollback path only runs when `rename(temp_path, db_path)` returns an error, so this still violates the non-destructive rebuild guarantee the change is meant to provide.

`semantic_dup_index_build` still deletes the live `db_path` when `extract_code_fragments` returns an empty set, so an empty or sparse workspace can wipe a previously working index instead of preserving it. That breaks the new non-destructive rebuild intent for `dup-index build` and makes a typo'd `--workspace-root` data-destructive.

The new rollback/swap behavior in `semantic_dup_index_build` is not exercised directly by any test in this file. The added unit tests only cover private helpers, so the public API's critical paths (stale-temp cleanup, crash-backup restore, and atomic commit failure handling) can regress without detection.

The guard uses `backup_path.exists()`, which returns false for broken symlinks. If a user already has a broken `.index.db.old` symlink, this block is skipped and the later `rename(db_path, backup_path)` overwrites that unrelated path instead of refusing to clobber it. Use `symlink_metadata`/`try_exists` on the backup path itself before deciding it is absent, then apply the recognizable-index check.

The tracked patch deletes the monolithic `semantic_dup.rs`, but the replacement `semantic_dup/` module files are untracked and therefore not present in `git diff`. A submitted checkout would have `mod semantic_dup;` with no tracked module file, breaking compilation and removing the semantic-dup DTOs/method implementations. Add the new module files to the patch or keep the original file tracked.

`search()` interprets LanceDB results as cosine similarity (`1.0 - _distance`, then clamp to `[0,1]`), but the query never sets `DistanceType::Cosine`. LanceDB `vector_search` defaults to L2, so this adapter will rank by L2 while reporting cosine-based scores, which can collapse most results to `0.0` and makes the returned similarities incorrect.

`search()` treats any `open_table()` failure as “table missing” and returns `Ok(Vec::new())`. That silently hides real storage errors such as permission failures, corruption, or schema problems; only `TableNotFound` should be converted to an empty result set.

`LanceDbSemanticIndexAdapter` calls `Runtime::block_on` in `new`, `insert`, and `search`. Tokio documents that `block_on` panics when invoked from an async execution context, so constructing or using this adapter from async code will crash instead of returning `SemanticIndexError`. That is a panic path in library code.

The first-insert path is a non-atomic `open_table(...); if missing -> create_table(...)` sequence. Because `insert` is `&self` and the adapter is `Send + Sync`, two callers can race on an empty database: both observe "table not found", one creates the table, and the other fails with a spurious `TableAlreadyExists`/`InsertFailed`, dropping the insert. Use LanceDB's create-if-exists mode or retry on table-exists.

`std::thread::spawn` panics if the OS cannot create a new thread, so this helper can crash the library instead of returning a `Result`. The same pattern also appears in `new()` at line 83, which means `insert`/`search` inherit an unrecoverable panic path under thread-exhaustion or spawn failure.

`to_string_lossy()` silently rewrites non-UTF-8 paths, so `new()` can open/create the wrong LanceDB directory on Unix, and `insert()` stores `source_path` values that cannot round-trip back to the original `PathBuf`. That loses user data for any fragment or database path containing invalid UTF-8 bytes.

A `current_thread` Tokio runtime is paired with `Handle::block_on` in both `new()` and `block_on_thread()`, but `Handle::block_on` does not drive a current-thread runtime. LanceDB's async `connect`/`open_table`/`add`/`search` futures will not make progress unless some thread is concurrently calling `Runtime::block_on`, so this adapter can hang or fail at startup and on every index operation.

`is_table_not_found` does not match LanceDB's actual missing-table error text (`Table '{name}' was not found`), so the fresh-database path never triggers. As a result, the intended first-run behavior in `insert` and `search` is broken: both will surface `InsertFailed`/`SearchFailed` instead of creating the table or returning an empty result set. Match `lancedb::Error::TableNotFound` directly instead of substring-searching `to_string()`.

`LanceDbSemanticIndexAdapter` owns a `tokio::runtime::Runtime` directly, but the adapter is explicitly made safe to construct and call from an existing Tokio executor by using bridge threads. If such an adapter is dropped inside that async context, Tokio's runtime destructor can panic while shutting down its blocking pool. This leaves a library-level panic path despite the no-panics rule; store the runtime in an `Option` and move/shutdown it from a non-runtime thread in `Drop`, or otherwise avoid owning a `Runtime` with normal in-context destruction.

`FastEmbedAdapter::new` does not honor the documented/plan-required `FASTEMBED_CACHE_PATH` cache override. The code uses `InitOptions::new(...)` without `with_cache_dir`, and fastembed 5.14's built-in lookup uses `FASTEMBED_CACHE_DIR`/`HF_HOME` instead, so CI or operators following this adapter's docs will have their pre-populated cache ignored and may trigger a runtime model download into the default cache location.

The round-trip test inserts `frag_b` with `emb_b` but never searches with `emb_b`, even though the test and briefing require verifying that each inserted fragment is returned as the top match for its own embedding. As written, a regression where only the first queried vector path works would still pass this coverage, so add the symmetric `adapter.search(&emb_b, TopK::new(2)...)` assertion for `src/b.rs`.

`is_item_boundary` still misses real Rust items like `extern "C" fn` / `pub extern "C" fn`: after consuming `extern`, the ABI string becomes the next token and the function boundary is not detected, so those files will not be split at the correct item start.

`expected_model_onnx_path()` is checking the wrong cache layout for fastembed/hf-hub. The real cache path for a populated model is `models--<repo>/snapshots/<commit>/onnx/model.onnx`, not `{cache}/models--<repo>/onnx/model.onnx`, and `pull_from_hf()` can also switch the cache root to `HF_HOME`. As written, `FastEmbedAdapter::new()` will reject a valid pre-populated cache instead of only failing when the model is actually absent, and the offline preflight does not reliably match the loader's real cache location.

`resolve_cache_dir()` only consults `FASTEMBED_CACHE_DIR`, but fastembed's loader path still honors `HF_HOME` when it is set. That means `FastEmbedAdapter::new()` can preflight one cache root and initialize `TextEmbedding` against another, so a valid offline cache may be rejected or a runtime fetch can still happen.

The new tests only exercise `model_snapshots_dir` / `model_onnx_exists_in_cache`; they never call `FastEmbedAdapter::new()` on an empty temp cache or a populated cache. The changed public behavior is therefore still unverified, which leaves the offline-preflight path free to regress silently.

This check can return true for any stale `snapshots/<commit>/onnx/model.onnx` directory, but hf-hub resolves the active file via `refs/<revision>` before looking in `snapshots/` (`hf-hub-0.5.0/src/lib.rs:141-147`). If the cached snapshot is not the one pointed to by `refs/main`, `FastEmbedAdapter::new()` will still fall through to `TextEmbedding::try_new()` and can hit the network. The offline preflight needs to validate the revision-selected snapshot, not just any snapshot.

Even when this preflight passes, `TextEmbedding::try_new()` still loads tokenizer/config assets from Hugging Face (`load_tokenizer_hf_hub(...)`), so a cache containing only `onnx/model.onnx` is not enough to keep `FastEmbedAdapter::new()` offline. That means the constructor can still perform runtime downloads unless the tokenizer files are also preflighted or the loader path is changed to be fully local-only.

Using `HF_HOME/hub` here does not match fastembed 5.14's `pull_from_hf`, which forwards `HF_HOME` itself as the cache dir. A cache populated under the actual `HF_HOME` root will be treated as missing and `FastEmbedAdapter::new()` will fail even though the model is present.

The offline preflight only checks `onnx/model.onnx` and `tokenizer.json`, but `TextEmbedding::try_new()` still calls `load_tokenizer_hf_hub()`, which fetches `config.json`, `special_tokens_map.json`, and `tokenizer_config.json` via `ApiRepo::get()` when they are absent. As written, `FastEmbedAdapter::new()` can still reach the network after the preflight passes, so the no-runtime-download guarantee is not enforced.

The new test assertions move `result` in `matches!(result, ...)` and then borrow it again in `result.err()` for the failure message. That is a use-after-move and this test module will not compile. The same pattern is repeated in the other two `new_with_cache_dir` tests.

The patch never exercises the public `FastEmbedAdapter::new()` path; all new tests call the private `new_with_cache_dir` helper instead. That leaves the env-resolution logic in `resolve_cache_dir()` and the actual no-download guarantee of the public constructor unverified, so a regression in the shipped API could still slip through CI.

Empty `HF_HOME` is treated as unset here, but `fastembed::pull_from_hf()` treats any set `HF_HOME` as authoritative. If `HF_HOME` is exported as an empty string, the preflight checks the fallback cache while the real loader uses the empty path, so `FastEmbedAdapter::new()` can still reach the network and violate the offline-only guarantee.

The `new()` test is vacuous: it only asserts when the constructor returns `Err`, so on a machine that already has a cache it passes without exercising the offline-failure path. That leaves the public constructor's no-download behavior unverified.

These assertions move `result` into `matches!` and then use `result.as_ref().err()` / `result.err()` in the formatted message, which is a use-after-move compile error. The new test module will not build until the error value is captured before the `matches!` call or the formatting is rewritten.

`resolve_cache_dir()` treats an empty `FASTEMBED_CACHE_DIR` as if it were unset, but fastembed itself treats a set-empty value as authoritative. With `FASTEMBED_CACHE_DIR=""`, `new()` will preflight `.fastembed_cache` while `TextEmbedding::try_new()` resolves the empty path, so the adapter can still fall through to a network download despite the supposed offline-only preflight.

`test_new_returns_error_when_no_env_vars_set` is not a deterministic test of the public constructor: it silently returns when `HF_HOME` or `FASTEMBED_CACHE_DIR` is set and it relies on the ambient working directory not containing a `.fastembed_cache` tree. That leaves the only `FastEmbedAdapter::new()` coverage environment-dependent, so regressions in `resolve_cache_dir()` or the constructor's env precedence can slip through CI.

The constructor tests do not actually cover `resolve_cache_dir()`'s env precedence. `new_with_cache_dir()` bypasses that logic entirely, and the only `new()` test assumes a clean ambient environment, so regressions in `HF_HOME` vs `FASTEMBED_CACHE_DIR` selection can still ship unnoticed.

There is no happy-path test for the public constructor after a pre-populated cache is present. Every added assertion only checks the `Err(ModelLoadFailed)` branch, so the code path that should still return `Ok(FastEmbedAdapter)` is unverified.

`test_new_returns_error_in_clean_ci_environment` is not hermetic: it assumes both `HF_HOME` and `FASTEMBED_CACHE_DIR` are unset and that no `.fastembed_cache` exists in the current working directory. On a developer machine or cached CI runner where any of those are present, `FastEmbedAdapter::new()` can take a different path or even succeed, so this regression test is flaky and does not reliably validate the public constructor's offline behavior.

The new constructor path is not tested directly: every test in this file exercises `new_with_cache_dir(...)` instead of the public `FastEmbedAdapter::new()`, so regressions in `resolve_cache_dir()` or the `with_cache_dir(...)` wiring would still pass the suite. The comment claiming end-to-end coverage for `new()` is therefore inaccurate.

`CodeFragment` documents and validates that `content` is always non-empty, but the `content` field is public, so callers can construct `CodeFragment { source_path, content: String::new() }` or clear it after `new()` succeeds, bypassing `SemanticDupError::EmptyContent`. This leaves the illegal empty-fragment state representable outside the constructor; keep the field private and expose read accessors if needed.
